<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Ōtataneko]][[ja:大田田根子]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Ōtataneko]][[ja:大田田根子]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Ōtataneko]][[ja:大田田根子]]







{{Short description|Japanese nobleman}}
{{Infobox royalty|thumb|240x240px||title=[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|image=File:Hibara-jinja, torii closeup.jpg|caption={{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} at the foot of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Miwa|qq=}} in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|lt=Sakurai|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai, Nara|qq=}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}. The shrine is identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after they were removed from the imperial palace.|successor=|house=[[Miwa clan|Miwa clan]]|father=a descendant of {{ill|Kamo no Okimi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Okimi|qq=}}}}
{{Expand Japanese}}

{{Nihongo|{{ill|Ōtataneko|ja|大田田根子|1=Ōtataneko|4=fr|5=Ōtataneko命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtataneko|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtataneko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to FR}}|太田田根子}} was a Japanese mythological figure<ref>{{Cite web |last=KansaiOdyssey |date=2017-07-26 |title=Oh Gods! The Tales of Omononushi |url=https://kansai-odyssey.com/oh-gods-the-tales-of-omononushi/ |access-date=2023-10-03 |website=Kansai Odyssey |language=en-US}}</ref> He is the traditional ancestor of the {{ill|Miwa clan|en|4=fr|5=Clan Miwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa clan|qq=}}.<ref name="chamberlain-basil-1882a" />

== Mythical narrative ==
{{Further|Mount Miwa|Ōmononushi|Ise Saiō}}
[[File:Hibara-jinja, torii closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|{{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}} at the foot of {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Miwa|qq=}} in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurai, Nara|qq=}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|WD=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}. The {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} is identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after they were removed from the imperial palace.]]
Amaterasu ({{ill|Shintai|lt=via the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi sword|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintai|qq=}}) and {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}, the {{ill|tutelary deity|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tutelary deity|qq=}} of Yamato, were originally worshipped in the great hall of the imperial palace.<ref name="aston-1896a">{{cite wikisource|year=1896|plaintitle=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wslink=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|wspages=151-154|chapter=Book I|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|author-first=William George|author-last=Aston|qq=draft}}</ref><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a">{{cite book |title=Kogoshūi: Gleanings from Ancient Stories. Translated with an introduction and notes |date=1925 |publisher=Meiji Japan Society |pages=29–30 |translator1-last=Katō |translator1-first=Genchi |translator2-last=Hoshino |translator2-first=Hikoshirō}}</ref>

{{ill|infection|Pestilence|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/infection|qq=}} struck during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|Sujin's|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Sujin|qq=}} rule, killing half the Japanese population. The following year peasants abandoned their fields and rebellion became rampant.<ref name="aston150–164">{{cite book |author=Aston, William George. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2 |date=1896 |publisher=The Japan Society London |isbn=9780524053478 |pages=150–164 |author-link=William George Aston}}</ref> To help relieve the suffering of his people, the Emperor turned his attention towards the gods. At the time, both the sun goddess {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}} and the god {{Nihongo|{{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}|倭大国魂神}}  were enshrined at the Imperial Residence. Sujin became overwhelmed with having to cohabit with these two powerful deities and set up separate enshrinements to house them. Amaterasu was moved to {{Nihongo|Kasanui village|笠縫邑}} in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Province|qq=}} (Nara), where a {{ill|Himorogi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himorogi|qq=}} altar was built out of solid stone.<ref name="aston150–164" />{{efn|During the reign of Sujin's son and successor, {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Suinin|qq=}}, custody of the sacred treasures were transferred from Toyosukiirihime to Suinin's daughter {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|lt=Yamatohime|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qq=}}, who took them first to "Sasahata in Uda" to the east of Miwa. Heading north to {{ill|Ōmi Province|Ōmi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}}, she then eastwards to {{ill|Mino Province|Mino|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mino Province|qq=}} and proceeded south to {{ill|Ise Province|Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Province|qq=}}, where she received a revelation from Amaterasu:

{{quotation|Now Ama-terasu no Oho-kami instructed Yamato-hime no Mikoto, saying:—"The province of Ise, of the divine wind, is the land whither repair the waves from the eternal world, the successive waves. It is a secluded and pleasant land. In this land I wish to dwell." In compliance, therefore, with the instruction of the Great Goddess, a shrine was erected to her in {{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province|1=Ise Province}}. Accordingly an {{ill|Abstinence Palace|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abstinence Palace|qq=}} was built at Kaha-kami in Isuzu. This was called the palace of Iso. It was there that Ama-terasu no Oho-kami first descended from Heaven.<ref>{{cite wikisource|author-first= William George |author-last= Aston |chapter= Book I |wslink= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |plaintitle= Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |year= 1896 |publisher= Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co.|wspages=176|qq=draft}}</ref>}}

This account serves as the origin myth of the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|Grand Shrine of Ise|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Grand Shrine|qq=}}, Amaterasu's chief place of worship.}} Sujin placed his daughter {{ill|Toyosukiiri-hime|ja|豊鍬入姫命|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyosukiiri-hime|qq=}} in charge of the new shrine, and she would become the first {{ill|Saiō|lt=Saiō|lt=Ise Saiō|en|4=fr|5=Saiō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Saiō|qq=}},<ref>{{cite web | url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8608 | title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 }}</ref>entrusting with her the mirror and sword, she brought  them to the village of Kasanuhi.<ref name="aston-1896a" /><ref name="meiji-japan-society-1925a" /> {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}} (the other god) was entrusted to another daughter named {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}, but her health began to fail shortly afterward. It is recorded that Nunakiiri-hime became emaciated after losing all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.<ref name="aston150–164" /> These events still did not alleviate the ongoing plague sweeping the empire, so Sujin decreed a {{ill|divination|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/divination|qq=}} to be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign. The divination involved him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji or {{Nihongo|Kamu-asaji-ga-hara|神浅茅原|qq=draft}}, and invoking the {{ill|Yaoyorozu no Kami|lt=eighty myriad deities|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yaoyorozu no Kami|qq=}}.<ref name="aston150–164" /> This worship of the deities is seen as being potentially linked to defining a more complex social order, and organizing the deities of many clans across the region.<ref>{{Citation |title=Contents |date=2017-12-31 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824862848-001 |work=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai |pages=189–191 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |doi=10.1515/9780824862848-001 |isbn=978-0-8248-6284-8}}</ref>

Sujin's aunt {{Nihongo|{{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|WD=Q11387098|lt=Yamatototohimomoso-hime|1=Yamato-totohi-momoso-hime}}|倭迹迹日百襲媛命|}} (daughter of 7th Emperor {{ill|Emperor Kōrei|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Kōrei|qq=}}) acted as a ''{{ill|miko|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/miko|qq=}}'', and was possessed by a god who identified himself as {{ill|Ōmononushi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=}},<ref name="aston150–164" /><ref name="ellwood-1990a">{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood |first=Robert S. |date=1990 |title=The Sujin Religious Revolution |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/30234018 |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=17 |issue=2/3 |pages=199–217 |doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217 |jstor=30234018 |issn=0304-1042|qq=draft}}</ref> possibly the same entity as {{ill|Yamato Okunitama|en|Yamato Okunitama|WD=Q11387080|lt=Yamato-no-Okunitama|1=Yamato Okunitama}}.  {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would conduct the Okunitama rites replacing the emaciated {{ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nunaki-iri-hime|qq=}}.<ref name="ellwood-1990a" /> {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the {{ill|Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|en|Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|WD=Q11387078|lt=Yamato clan|1=Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208 - Wikisource, the free online library |url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208 |access-date=2023-10-24 |website=en.wikisource.org |language=en}}</ref> This replacement is taken as a shift towards more patriarchai religion.<ref name="ellwood-1990a" /> This god claimed responsibility for the plague, announcing that it would not stop until he was venerated. Although the Emperor propitiated to the god, the effects were not immediate. Sujin was later given guidance in the form of a dream to seek out a man named {{Nihongo|{{ill|Ōtataneko|ja|大田田根子|1=Ōtataneko|4=fr|5=Ōtataneko命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtataneko|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtataneko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to FR}}|太田田根子}} and appoint him as head priest.<ref name="aston150–164" /> He eventually found him in {{ill|Izumo Province|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=}}.<ref name="ellwood-1990a" /> When he was found and installed, the pestilence eventually subsided, allowing five cereal crops to ripen.<ref name="aston150–164" /> Out of an abundance of caution, the Emperor also appointed {{Nihongo|{{ill|Ikagashikoo|ja|伊香色雄|1=Ikagashiko-o|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ikagashikoo|en:draft|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikagashikoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikagashiko-o|qq=}}|伊香色雄|1=Ikagashiko-o}} as {{Nihongo|''kami-no-mono-akatsu-hito''|神班物者}}, or one who sorts the offerings to the gods.<ref name="chamberlain-basil-1882a">{{cite book |author=Chamberlain, Basil |url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj072.htm |title=''[SECT. LXV.—EMPEROR SŪ-JIN (PART III: STORY OF OHO-TATA-NE-KO'S BIRTH)]'' (''The Kojiki'') |publisher=Read before the Asiatic Society of Japan on April 12, May 10, and June 21, 1882, reprinted in 1919 |page=219 |quote=His Augustness Oho-tata-ne-ko&nbsp;... was the ancestor of the Dukes of Miwa and of the Dukes of Kamo. |author-link=Basil Chamberlain}}</ref> To this day the {{ill|Miwa clan|lt=Miwa sept|en|4=fr|5=Clan MIwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa clan|qq=}} of the {{ill|Kamo clan|en|4=fr|5=Clan Kamo|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo clan|qq=}} claim to be descents from {{ill|Ōtataneko|ja|大田田根子|1=Ōtataneko|4=fr|5=Ōtataneko命|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtataneko|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtataneko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to FR}}, while {{ill|Ikagashikoo|ja|伊香色雄|1=Ikagashiko-o|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ikagashikoo|en:draft|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikagashikoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikagashiko-o|qq=}} was a claimed ancestor of the now extinct {{ill|Mononobe clan|en|4=fr|5=Clan Mononobe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe clan|qq=}}.<ref name="chamberlain-basil-1882a" /> This has been suggested as representing a population migration from {{ill|Izumo Province|Izumo|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824862848 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai |date=2017-12-18 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6284-8 |pages=152–168|doi=10.1515/9780824862848 }}</ref>

==Genealogy==
Ōtataneko is a descendant of {{ill|Kamo no Okimi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamo no Okimi|qq=}} displayed here{{Eight generations of Izumo}}
== See Also ==

* {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiwa Shrine|qq=}}
* {{ill|Miwa clan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miwa clan|qq=}}
* {{ill|Hotsuma Tsutae|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hotsuma Tsutae|qq=}}
==Notes==
{{notelist}}

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

{{Miwa and Yamato Faith}}

{{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}}
<!--removing this template because now we do not need it. All everybodywiki edit histories have been imported-->




{{everybodywiki link|Ōtataneko}}

{{everybodywiki link|Ōtataneko|Ōtataneko}}

[[fr:Utilisateur:Immanuelle/Ōtataneko]]

<!--all this actually mandates is a completion tier 7 ranking. We are not using the promising draft template here. If I get anywhere near implementing it, I will do so based on the actual completion tiers of this wiki rather than whether I added them when they were on simple english wikipedia or wherever-->


[[ja:大田田根子]]
{{translated page|ja|大田田根子|version=105473390|comment=Imported full ja history}}


{{wikidata link|Q55536798}}
[[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
[[Category:Need translation]]
